---
title: "The Audit Pipeline: Making Your Adjustments Defensible"
---

When you run your data through SPARMVET, three "tiers" are presented:

- **T1 — Assembled**: the raw data joined per your manifest. No analytical decisions made yet.
- **T2 — Analysis-ready**: the standard transformations that every plot in your project shares.
- **T3 — My adjustments**: *your* decisions on top of T2. This is where you remove bad samples, drop unused columns, filter to the rows you want to publish.

The audit pipeline is what records every T3 decision so your final figures are reproducible and defensible. Every change you make in T3 mode is captured as an **audit node** — a small record that says *what* you changed, *which plots* it applies to, and (mandatory) *why* you made the change. Together, the audit nodes form your audit trail.

## Why this matters for publication

When you submit a paper, a reviewer or editor may ask: "Why did you remove sample S2?" Without the audit pipeline, your answer is "I remember… I think it was the contamination." With the audit pipeline, the answer is in the exported report:

> ⚠️ **[Primary key affected]** Excluded sample `S2` from all plots (reason: poor sequencing quality, see lab notebook entry 2026-04-12).

Every removal is named, dated, and justified. You take responsibility for the decision; the report makes that responsibility visible.

## How to author audit decisions

In **T3 ("My adjustments")** mode, the left sidebar is your only authoring surface:

- The **filter builder** (Apply button labelled "➜ Audit (N)" in orange) — turn the rows you've staged into audit nodes.
- The **column selector above the data preview** ("➜ Audit drops (N)" button) — turn deselected columns into `drop_column` audit nodes.

The right sidebar is the **audit panel**: it shows the audit decisions for the *currently active plot*. Every decision lives there with its reason text-box. Until you fill in a reason, the bottom **Apply** button stays locked. Once you Apply, the decisions move from "pending" to "committed" and the change is reflected in the data.

To remove an audit decision, click the 🗑 next to it. The data immediately reappears (audit nodes only ever *remove* data; deleting a node is the only "undo").

## Per-plot vs. all-plots: the propagation choice

Most adjustments only make sense on one plot. If you filter "value > 90" on a similarity plot, that doesn't apply to your QC plot — they have different data. So by default, every audit decision is **per-plot only**. Switching plots in T3 mode shows you a different audit panel.

**But sometimes a decision should propagate.** A clear example: if sample `S2` has poor sequencing quality, you'll want to remove it from every plot — except possibly the QC contamination plot, which is precisely where you'd point a reviewer to *show* that S2 was bad.

When you author an audit decision that's eligible for propagation, SPARMVET shows you a small dialog with three choices:

- **This plot only** — the default, fastest path. Applies just to the plot you're currently looking at.
- **All plots** — the decision is copied into every plot's audit panel. New plots added to your manifest later will *not* automatically inherit this decision; you'd need to re-propagate.
- **All plots except…** — pick which plots to skip. This is the "remove S2 everywhere except the QC contamination plot" case.

Linked deletion: if you've propagated to multiple plots and later click 🗑 on the node in *any* of those plots, the node is removed from *all* of them. The audit pipeline thinks of a propagated node as one decision applied many places, not many independent decisions.

### Propagation preview — read it before you confirm

Before you click *Add to audit pipeline*, the modal shows a per-plot preview of where the filter will actually land:

> 📍 **Propagation preview:** ✅ 5 apply  •  ⚠️ 2 skip (col missing)
>
> ▶ Show details
>   ⚠️ *Plasmid Prevalence by Country* — column not in plot data; will be SKIPPED
>   ⚠️ *Phenotype Genome Length* — column not in plot data; will be SKIPPED
>
> 👉 Apply filters one at a time and verify each plot before stacking. A skipped plot is **NOT filtered** — it shows the unfiltered data.

**Three states per target plot:**

- ✅ **column present** → the filter will apply
- ⚠️ **column absent** → the filter is silently skipped on that plot (the plot shows unfiltered data; verify it's what you want)
- ❓ **dataset unknown** → SPARMVET couldn't resolve that plot's data; verify by hand after applying

**Why "apply one at a time"?** When you author multiple filters and propagate them in batch, every skipped plot becomes a question: *did the user mean to skip it, or did they not realise their filter wouldn't take effect there?* By applying one filter, switching tabs, and verifying the effect before authoring the next, you build an honest, defensible audit trail.

## Primary keys — extra care needed

A **primary key** is a column that links your tables together. In a typical SPARMVET project the primary key is `sample_id`; in long-format AMR data, gene identifiers can also serve as join keys. SPARMVET detects primary keys automatically from your manifest's join recipes.

When you target a primary key with an audit decision, SPARMVET applies extra rules:

### Dropping a primary-key column is **blocked**

You can't add a `drop_column` node for `sample_id` (or any other join key). The link between your tables would break. If you click "➜ Audit drops" with a primary key in the deselected list, you'll see:

> Column `sample_id` is a join key — cannot be dropped. Use a row filter or row exclusion instead.

If your need is to *anonymize* the IDs in the published report (e.g. the journal asks you not to expose internal sample IDs), that is a different problem and currently outside SPARMVET's scope. For now, the report shows whatever IDs your manifest uses; consider relabelling samples *before* assembly if anonymization is a hard requirement.

### Filtering rows on a primary key is **allowed** (with a warning)

You *can* filter on a primary-key column — for example `sample_id == "S2"` to keep only that sample. SPARMVET keeps the node as a regular `filter_row` and **does not silently convert it** to an exclusion. The semantics are: the operator does exactly what it says (`eq` keeps the matched value; `ne`/`not_in` excludes it).

A ⚠️ **Primary key** banner is added to the node automatically, both in the modal and in the right-sidebar audit panel, to remind you that this decision changes which samples flow into joined plots downstream.

If your intent is *exclusion* ("remove S2 from everything"), use `ne` or `not_in` directly — the audit will then read as "rows removed where `sample_id == S2`" which is exactly the right framing for a methods section.

> Earlier behaviour (pre-2026-04-30) silently converted `eq`/`in` on a PK to `ne`/`not_in` — that turned "select X" into "remove X" without a clear UI cue and was confusing. The current rule is: **the operator is the truth**; the warning is the safety rail.

### The primary-key warning is permanent

Every audit node that targeted a primary key carries a `⚠️ Primary key — Primary ID/Key alignment` banner in the audit panel. The same warning persists into the exported report:

> ⚠️ **[Primary key affected]** Excluded sample `S2` from all plots…

This is **not** an error or a complaint — it's a marker for you and your readers: "this decision changes which samples appear across joined plots; the user owns this decision". It's expected to be present in many real audit reports.

## When *not* to use a primary-key exclusion

Sometimes the user's instinct is "remove sample S2 from this plot" when the actual analytical intent is different. Common case:

> "I'm looking at a long-format gene-similarity table. I only want similarities ≥ 90%. Sample S2 doesn't have any genes meeting that bar so I'll just exclude S2."

Don't do that. The correct framing is: *filter on the `value` column where `value >= 90`*. The effect on the visualization may be the same (S2 disappears) but the audit trail is meaningfully different:

- **Wrong**: "Excluded sample S2." → reader thinks S2 was a bad sample.
- **Right**: "Filtered to similarity ≥ 90." → reader understands S2 simply had no similarities meeting the threshold.

The first hides analytical context the reader needs. The second is honest. SPARMVET's automatic conversion of primary-key filters to exclusions only triggers when you explicitly target the key column — so as long as you build your filter on the `value` column instead, no conversion happens.

## Use cases summary

To make the rules concrete, these are the cases the design accommodates:

**Case A — Bad sample, but keep it on the plot that justifies the removal.**
Sample `S2` had poor quality. You want it gone from your analytical figures, *but* you want the QC contamination plot to still show S2's high contamination bar so your report can point to that plot as evidence.
→ Author the exclusion → propagate to **All plots except…** → select the QC plot in the multiselect.

**Case B — Wrong sample, remove everywhere.**
You discover S2 was mis-labelled or contaminated upstream. It shouldn't be in any plot.
→ Author the exclusion → propagate to **All plots**.

**Case C — Long-format value filter (not a sample-ID exclusion).**
Your long-format table has rows like `(sample, gene, similarity)`. You want similarity ≥ 90.
→ Build the filter on the `similarity` column, not on `sample`. Per-plot scope is fine. No primary-key warning fires (similarity isn't a join key). If S2 ends up with no rows, the audit correctly says "filtered to similarity ≥ 90" rather than "excluded S2".

## Saving and restoring your work

Every time you click **Apply**, your committed audit decisions are saved to a **T3 ghost** file alongside the assembly results. If you close SPARMVET and come back later with the same manifest and the same data files, the ghost is restored automatically — your audit decisions reappear in their per-plot panels exactly as you left them.

If your data files have changed since the last session, SPARMVET notices and warns you: the assembly is rebuilt, but the audit decisions persist (a row filter on `sample_id == S2` keeps applying as long as `sample_id` exists in the new data; the audit pipeline doesn't care that the assembly was redone).

If you restructure your manifest — rename a plot, remove a plot — any audit nodes that target the missing plot become **orphaned**. SPARMVET tells you about them at restore time and lists them in an "Orphaned" section so you can re-target or delete them. They never apply silently; you always make a deliberate choice.

## The bottom line

The audit pipeline is built around one principle: **every analytical decision should be defensible, traceable, and named**. T3 mode is where you turn raw data into the figures that tell your scientific story; the audit pipeline is the receipts file that proves you didn't fabricate or over-massage that story.

The cost is: filling in a reason every time you remove a sample or drop a column. The payoff is: when a reviewer asks "why did you exclude S2", you can hand them the exported report and the answer is right there, in your own words, with the timestamp.

---

## Appendix: Filter operators reference

The filter builder in the left sidebar adapts its widgets to the column type:

### Numeric columns (`Int*`, `Float*`, `Decimal`)

| Op       | Symbol | Widget                | Polars semantics                       |
|----------|--------|-----------------------|----------------------------------------|
| `eq`     | `=`    | numeric input         | `col == value`                         |
| `ne`     | `≠`    | numeric input         | `col != value`                         |
| `gt`     | `>`    | numeric input         | `col > value`                          |
| `ge`     | `≥`    | numeric input         | `col >= value`                         |
| `lt`     | `<`    | numeric input         | `col < value`                          |
| `le`     | `≤`    | numeric input         | `col <= value`                         |
| `between`| `↔`    | two numeric inputs (min, max) + inclusivity radio | `col.is_between(lo, hi, closed=...)` |

The **`between`** operator has an inclusivity toggle:

- `≤ ≤ inclusive` (default) → `closed='both'` → audit shows `col ∈ [lo, hi]`
- `< < exclusive`           → `closed='none'` → audit shows `col ∈ (lo, hi)`

If the user enters `lo > hi`, SPARMVET auto-swaps them rather than producing an empty filter.

### Categorical / discrete columns

| Op        | Symbol | Widget                  | Polars semantics                |
|-----------|--------|-------------------------|---------------------------------|
| `in`      | `∈`    | selectize multi-pick    | `col.is_in([v1, v2, ...])`      |
| `not_in`  | `∉`    | selectize multi-pick    | `~col.is_in([v1, v2, ...])`     |
| `eq`      | `=`    | selectize multi-pick    | (auto-promoted to `in` when multiple values selected) |
| `ne`      | `≠`    | selectize multi-pick    | (auto-promoted to `not_in` when multiple values selected) |

Set-membership comparisons cast both sides to string, so a numeric column declared `discrete` (via `scale_x_discrete` in the manifest) still works with the selectize picker.

### Auto-promotion rules

The filter row stores a single `op` field but the underlying logic adapts when the value is a list:

- `op="eq"` with a list → upgraded to `op="in"`
- `op="ne"` with a list → upgraded to `op="not_in"`

This is intentional — picking multiple values from the selectize implies set membership.

### Type coercion (defensive)

Numeric column + string operand (e.g. user typed `"2022"` for a Float64 year column) — the apply-time coercion converts the string to the column's native dtype. Both filter paths (data preview and plot render) do this identically.

If a string operand reaches a numeric column despite the dtype-aware widgets, a warning fires in the server log:

```
[filter] ⚠️ string operand on numeric column 'year' (Float64); coercing '2022'
```

This means the widget bypassed its own type contract; not user-facing but worth flagging in dev.

### Regression test

The end-to-end operator contract is locked down by `app/tests/test_filter_operators.py` (21 cases covering every op × value-shape × dtype combination). Run from the repo root:

```bash
pytest app/tests/test_filter_operators.py -v
```
